text: Sometimes we have to do repetitions in javascript. For example, if I tell you to print numbers from 1 to 10, you will do it by doing console.log. But if I tell you to do it from 1 to 1000, or from 1 to 1 billion, then you may not be able to do it. And for this, loops are used in javascript. To automate your repetitive tasks. How is the syntax? What is for loop? What is while loop? What is do while? Along with this, we are going to see for in and for off loop in this video. Like this video and write sigma patch op in the comment section below. Let's go to the computer screen. Let's roll the intro. So as I told you, if I tell you to print numbers from 1 to 10, then you may do it by doing console.log. 1, 2, 3, 4, all the way till 10. But if I tell you to print numbers from 1 to 1000, then you may not be able to do it. So to explain this quickly, I will make a folder named 57. And I will open it in VS code. And I guess my 56 is also open. I will close it. And here I will quickly make a file named index.js. Note that I am making a javascript file here. I am not making an HTML file. But I can put all the code in an HTML script tag. As long as I am using all those things which are available in my browser. But now because we are learning loops, so I will quickly put a console.log here. And I will write here, I am a tutorial on loops. Okay, let me zoom in a little so that you can see. And as soon as I run it, it will open in the terminal here. If I want, I can also make its tab, which I personally prefer. And after this, you see, I am a tutorial on loops here. You are getting to see. Now look at one thing here. If I write here, let a is equal to one. And here I write console.log a. Then after that I write console.log a plus one. Then I write console.log a plus two. There is a limit as long as I can do this. I will get tired, my program will become so big. Maybe my editor will hang if I write a lot of millions of lines. So I don't want my editor to hang. And the JavaScript engine also doesn't want that my editor hangs. That's why JavaScript has a loop construct. What does the loop construct do? It helps you to do any of your tasks, repetitive tasks again and again. We have five types of loops in JavaScript. There is a for loop, which is a normal for loop. One is a for in loop, one is a for off loop. And after this, there is a while loop and one is a do while loop. Let's see how these loops work. First of all, I will show you a very basic for loop. If you want to do this, I will run this, you will see one, two, three. But let's say I want to print up to 100 and I will show you now. If you do it manually, one, two, three, four, five, six, you will keep writing a plus two, a plus three, a plus four, a plus five. You will get tired. And I don't want you to get tired. JavaScript also doesn't want you to get tired. That's why it made a loop construct. So you say a is equal to one. And after this, you put a for loop here. Now see, as soon as I wrote for here, you see, notice this thing. Here is a for, a for each, a for in, a for off. So there is a for each loop, which we will see later. If you change it, it will change one, two, three, four places. Suppose I only prefer i. Or suppose I only prefer a. I kept i. So wherever i was, it changed there. Now I will press the tab key on my keyboard. I have pressed the tab key. Oops, I will press the tab key on the keyboard. But because I don't want to access the length of the array, nor am I dealing with the array right now, so I will put a number here. Which will be, let us say, 100. And I will write here, console.lock a plus i. So the value of i will vary all the way from zero to 100. Okay? So here I have ruined its closing. When I run it and show you, then see it printed from 1 to 100. But how did it do it? First of all, what happens is that it runs this part of the code. Only once, when the loop is initialized, this is called initialization. So let i is equal to zero, what will it do? It will make the value of i zero. Then after this comes the condition, which is checked. Is i less than 100? If i is less than 100, then it will enter the loop, otherwise it will not enter. Now because i is less than 100, zero is less than 100, then it will enter the loop. And here it will execute console.log a plus i. Which will do what? 1 plus 100, 101, sorry 1 plus zero. The value of i was zero now. 1 plus zero, which was 1, it will print it. The value of a plus i is also 1. After that, what will it do? It will run this and now the value of i will become 1.
